title: How the Oil Price Gets Made: Benchmarks, the Cost Curve & the Marginal Barrel
quick_definition: The oil price isn't one number handed down — it's made, in physical and paper markets, around a handful of benchmarks, and it gravitates to the cost of the marginal barrel: the most expensive barrel the world needs to pump to meet demand.
explainer_covers: The benchmarks. WTI (US light crude, priced at Cushing, Oklahoma) and Brent (international waterborne crude) are the two oil references; Henry Hub is the US natural-gas benchmark (★G308). The load-bearing distinction: oil is a global price (it ships anywhere, so one world price rules), while gas is regional (hard to move without liquefaction, so US, European, and Asian gas prices diverge) — which is why LNG matters and why gas isn't just "cheap oil."

Physical vs paper. The price you see is set as much in futures (NYMEX) as in physical cargoes — far more oil trades on paper than is ever delivered (Blas & Farchy's "paper barrels"). The futures curve can be contango (future price above spot — storage economics) or backwardation (spot above future — scarcity now). Contango/backwardation and the mechanics of the curve are named here but belong to a future derivatives module — not taught in depth.

The cost curve (the sector's master diagram). Stack the world's supply from cheapest to dearest: Gulf/Saudi barrels at the bottom (single-digit-to-teens $/bbl lifting cost), then Russia and conventional fields, US shale in the middle, and deepwater, oil sands & Arctic at the top. Demand draws a vertical line; where it crosses the curve is the marginal barrel, and that barrel's cost anchors the price. This is Z1.2's "cost position" made visual — and it's why the low-cost producer is unkillable.

Why the price is so violent. Short-run demand is inelastic (you can't stop commuting when gasoline spikes; you can't instantly electrify a truck fleet) and short-run supply is inelastic too (barrels take time). So a small supply-demand gap forces a large price move to clear the market — the mechanical root of the cycle in Z3.2.
The gauges. Inventories (are stocks building or drawing?) and spare capacity (Z3.3) are the two dials analysts read to see which way the imbalance — and the price — is tilting.
Priced in dollars. Crude is quoted and settled in USD, so the dollar's level is an input to the oil price — the seam into macro-economics.z5.5.

connects_to.note: spare capacity — the cartel's lever on this price

connects_to_raw: Z1.2 (cost position → the cost curve) · Z3.2 (inelasticity → the cycle) · Z3.3 (spare capacity — the cartel's lever on this price) · `macro-economics.z5.5` (the dollar that prices oil) · `hedge-funds.z2.4` / `z2.5` (macro desks trading the benchmark).

gaps.note: oil101-extraction-poor — Oil 101, the market-plumbing primer, came in with broken OCR; the benchmark/curve/trading-plumbing detail is drawn from Mu Ch. 7 and The World for Sale and is sound, but citation depth on the physical-trading mechanics is lighter than Tier A intends. Severity: low-medium.

real_world_layer: Mu Ch. 7; Blas & Farchy paper barrels; WTI/Brent/Henry Hub.

title: The Oil Cycle: Boom, Bust & the Machinery You Already Know
quick_definition: Energy is a cyclical sector, and its cycle is not a new object — it's the corpus's cycle machinery with a commodity accent: high prices summon capital, capital summons supply years too late, the glut breaks the price, and underinvestment sows the next shortage.
explainer_covers: The loop, built on inherited parts. A high price → a capex boom → new supply that arrives years later (a deepwater project sanctioned today produces late this decade — this is ★G232's supply-lag structure, borrowed wholesale from real estate) → oversupply → price crash → capex slashed → underinvestment → eventual shortage → recovery. Same pendulum as ★G127, same lag as ★G232.
Demand rides the business cycle. Oil demand tracks industrial activity and mobility, so recessions gut it — the demand side is ★G237, no new teaching required. The cycle is supply lag × demand swing.

Credit is the amplifier. Cheap capital in the boom over-funds marginal (often shale) producers; when the price breaks, the leverage detonates — the 2015–16 E&P high-yield bust is ★G255 wearing a hard hat. The financeability of drilling is part of the cycle.

Shale bent the cycle. Short-cycle shale (Z2.1) responds in months, not years, so US producers now act as a fast swing supply that caps price spikes and shortens cycles — a genuine structural change to the old megaproject rhythm, not just another turn.

Capital discipline — the current regime. After destroying capital from 2014–2020, the majors and E&Ps switched from drill-for-growth to return-the-cash — dividends and buybacks (★G42), production held flat on purpose. Whether this "discipline" survives the next high price is the sector's live question (also Z4.1). Taught at node level; below the backlog bar to mint.

The macro seam (never re-taught). The oil→inflation→rates transmission lives in Macro (z3.7 stagflation, z3.3 cost-push, z1.8 AS shift, ★G252/★G253/★G259). Z3.2 supplies the supply-side half — why the barrel got scarce — and back-links; it does not restate the canon.

connects_to.note: one-way — giving the demand-shock canon its supply-side counterpart

connects_to_raw: ★G127 · ★G237 · ★G232 · ★G255 (the four inherited engines this node runs on) · `macro-economics.z3.7` (one-way — giving the demand-shock canon its supply-side counterpart) · ★G42 (buybacks — the capital-returns regime) · Z2.4 (the cycle, amplified through services) · `hedge-funds.z2.5` (who trades it).

title: OPEC & the Visible Hand: Spare Capacity, Quotas & the Swing Producer
quick_definition: Oil is not a free market. A producer cartel — OPEC, now OPEC+ with Russia — coordinates output to steer the price, and its ability to do so rests on spare capacity: idle production it can switch on or off.
explainer_covers: What OPEC is and does. A group of producing states (Saudi Arabia the anchor) that sets production quotas to manage supply, controlling a large share of world output and nearly all the world's usable spare capacity (★G309). OPEC+ extended the tent to Russia and others. This is the "visible hand" that makes energy unlike a textbook commodity market.

Spare capacity — the shock absorber. The barrels a country could produce but isn't (overwhelmingly Saudi) are the global buffer: released, they cap a spike; withheld, they defend a price. Spare capacity is the single most-watched supply gauge (the other dial to Z3.1's inventories).

The swing producer. Whoever holds spare capacity can move the marginal barrel and thus the price — historically Saudi Arabia, increasingly contested by US shale's short-cycle swing (Z3.2). Two swing forces now share the wheel.

The cartel's inherent flaw. Quotas only work if members honor them, and the incentive is always to cheat (pump over quota while others restrain) — the classic cartel instability that periodically cracks OPEC discipline.

Price war vs price defense (the strategic switch). OPEC oscillates between defending price (cut output) and defending market share (pump hard to drive high-cost rivals out) — the 1986, 2014 and 2020 episodes were partly aimed at bankrupting US shale. Yergin's oil-shock and price-war history is the narrative spine.
Why an analyst tracks it. OPEC meeting outcomes are a standing critical factor (★G191) for any energy forecast — a scheduled, market-moving supply decision no other sector has an equivalent of.

connects_to.note: spare capacity → the price it sets
